video_title: Crypto's Biggest Problem ft. Sam Newman of Defi.com
transcript: The undercolateralized loan part of finance is missing from DeFi and it's [music] a big hole that we don't really talk about and it's also a big part of the usability and that means >> super happy to be hosting Sam Newman of defi.com. Why do you think that product is the way in for DeFi? product is the way that DeFi is going to mature because crypto is now going through this maturity cycle where it's moving away from just pure speculation into >> talk to me a bit about this spectrum of decentralization to centralization >> the decentralized finance is going [music] to be a lot bigger than DeFi as we know it today and at the moment the only real barrier to entry is >> excellent um super happy to be hosting Sam Newman of defi.com on the decipher podcast today. Uh Sam, I love to get into these conversations, you know, get straight into these conversations in in a big way. So, I noticed that your background is all kind of product focused, product management, um product development. And when you kind of check out defi.com, you very very much see this kind of product focus, strong aesthetic identity, strong product identity. Um, crypto is kind of experiencing a bit of an identity crisis and an attention crisis. Why do you think that product is the way in for DeFi? >> Yeah, hi Jack and uh thank you for having me. It's really really good to be here. Um, yeah, my my background is is in product primarily across both traditional finance, DeFi and and crypto infrastructure. [gasps] I think that to your question, product is the the way that DeFi is going to mature, right? And and crypto in in in more general terms because we're now moving to the stage of consolidation in the industry. Um there's some big players coming in from traditional finance and the web two landscape and they are buying up and acquiring some of the crypto infrastructure companies and product is is what's going to take what has been a a feature or a concept that's been very difficult for people to access and make it accessible and actually connect it to the real world. So we're now seeing obviously the increased adoption of stable coins and that's being driven primarily around remittances and also payments with with crypto cards. This is real world use case problems that are being solved by good products. And crypto is now going through this kind of maturity cycle where it's moving away from just pure speculation into something that is solving problems for people in in the real world and actually interacting with real world goods and services. So it's it's pretty exciting. There's some really good products coming through now and I think that we're we're in a good place. >> Defi.com is is kind of, you know, a broad umbrella. Congrats on on getting the domain name as well. Um but uh you know how do you would you describe to kind of a lay person what you guys do over there um you know around kind of just around I guess product? >> Yeah. So so yeah defi.com is a great great domain. It's got great a great kind of brand that goes with it that's been been worked out with the team here and we're we're building a strong brand identity around defi.com. We want it to be what we're calling the home of DeFi for people and that means all people. it it doesn't just mean cryptonative people and the products that we are building are going to be targeted at I would say digital native people rather than cryptonative people. So you can expect from defi.com to have a product that is going to feel closer to a fintech than maybe a a pure cryptonative defi app. And we want to make DeFi accessible to to everyone because there's lots of benefits of self- custody. There's lots of benefits in DeFi that you don't get in traditional finance. And at the moment, the the only real barrier to entry is a knowledge gap that the everyday person doesn't have or a level of intimidation that they may feel when they go to access D5 products. and we want to make that as simple as possible for people to to overcome and be able to get into some of these these DeFi protocols. So, what we're building is basically a consumerf facing app for people to manage their funds. They'll be able to earn on those funds through blue chip defy protocol yields. They'll also be able to spend their funds with a Visa card and access actual real world assets, goods and services. By doing that, they'll be able to swap tokens very easily with the best UX and also invest in real world tokenized assets as well. And on top of that, we are making sure that the table states requirements that people expect around privacy are maintained throughout. So what people see when they do what they don't see but what is under the hood with traditional finance is a level of privacy and when they move to onchain to a public blockchain and a a public ledger so to speak they lose that privacy and we want to bring that back. I think if we were rebuilding some of these blockchains from scratch and we would be building it with more privacy waiting in the in the thinking of the architecture and we want to make sure that that privacy is maintained particularly if we expect people to manage their finances on chain and um rely on some of these DeFi products going forward. It seems like you guys would it be fair to characterize you guys as a kind of like neo bank and and if so why is it important to kind of uh include you know payment use cases, yield use cases as well as kind of the speculative use cases that other you know generalist DeFi apps have kind of uh always supported >> because I think it goes back to that that question that you asked at the beginning around the the next kind of maturity cycle for DeFi. And we want to connect DeFi to the real world. And ultimately that comes to comes down to spending money and buying goods and services and using the hopefully the the gains that you've made from your assets that you've got invested in DeFi protocols and actually converting that and realizing that in the real world. So, it's important that we cover the full gamut of financial use cases all the way from um some speculation, although I don't I wouldn't say that we are um primarily focused on that, but we do want people to be able to access some of those products all the way through to the real world spend as well whilst maintaining that that privacy. So um yeah we are we are the home of looking to become the home of decentralized finance but decentralized finance is going to be a lot bigger than DeFi as we know it today. >> You also mentioned that you guys kind of focus on a on not someone who's cryptonative but someone who's digitally native. Uh I think that's interesting because you know a trope I hear interviewing founders sometimes is you know oh we want to onboard your grandma or we want to onboard your mom. Um, I think it's interesting that you key into this still kind of digitally native person. You're not trying to onboard maybe, you know, all seven 8 billion people, but you're trying to onboard, you know, the six billion people that that kind of are already onboard onboarded to, you know, [snorts] smartphones and, you know, are already doing online banking and stuff. Why is this distinction important to you guys between kind of cryptonative, digitally native, and someone who's kind of non non digitally native at all? Well, I I think the first answer around, you know, onboarding people's grandma is a little bit hyperbolic. I mean, your grandma would be very welcome, Jack. Everyone else's grandma is is welcome to use it as well, but when you're trying to build products, you do have to have a [snorts] specific type of user in mind. And spanning all age gaps and levels of digital fluency makes things more difficult. So, it would be easier for someone who's non-digitally native to on board to defy.com than it would be for them to spin up their own self-custodial wallet and um you know deposit some funds into a for example. But when we're thinking about our ideal target user, we are not trying to um explain every nuance to someone that's not used to using, for example, Revolute or Monzo or a fintech app. We want to target those kind of users, make it feel like a familiar experience because ultimately they are going to be a growing cohort of users and the people that are not digitally native will be will be moving on and we we're going to target those users. one of the primary distinctions between you guys and and incumbents or I guess one of the kind of two categories these you know neo bank fintexs kind of fall into is the cryptonative ones and then the digitally native ones that are onboarding you know crypto features right you know you mentioned some of them revolute PayPal why do you think that the kind of like crypto first self-custody first projects companies have a competitive advantage because I think that they are built from the ground up for this use case and this use case is going to be taking up a bigger slice of the pie going forward. So, think of Amazon, for example, was built from the ground up for e-commerce, right? And we still have a load of um bookshops, for example, that are now selling online, but they're not as competitive um on the e-commerce side. they live in parallel and I think that these two kind of um tracks that you mentioned will continue to live in parallel for for a long time but they're tackling a similar problem from two different angles and they will have a different a slightly different set of users but the way that we're building is from the ground up non-custodial E5 first um rather than taking it from like Revolute's point of view who are looking at this initially as um a side product and now they're starting to invest in it more heavily and gaining expertise in that area. Everything that we build will be from the ground up to to focus on the the crypto um rails approach. >> In some cases, I think originally in kind of like the early earliest crypto eras, we had kind of like the ideologically aligned crypto users come on. You know, they were super focused on decentralization. Recently we've seen a whole new wave of success around products that kind of put uh decentralization aside and kind of these ideological you know crypto cipher punk principles. They put these things to the side in favor of better product. you kind of are advocating for a mix of, you know, the best product and crypto rails enabling the best product, but also, you know, protecting the user and and bringing them the kind of use cases that are allowed by self-custody. Um, where why have you kind of decided to to fit in this spectrum? Um are there any challenges associated with kind of building decentralized first and you know over building a a kind of you know more kind of traditional interface that's just kind of crypto enabled. Talk to me a bit about this kind of like spectrum of decentralization to to centralization. >> Yeah, I mean it's it is a um a nuanced part and it's it's slightly loaded particularly kind of in in the crypto industry, right? People want things to be decentralized. they want self-custody. I think in reality and the approach that we're taking is maybe a bit more pragmatic in that regard because we do want people to be able to inter have their funds interact with the with the real world and ultimately for the most part at least in the the medium term that involves fiat currencies and anywhere where we are touching fiat currencies we fall into regulation and centralized entities that are are potentially um going to be doing some of applying some of that regulation So I would think of it like this. You you have a central core which is decentralized non-custodial and then on the fringes of that you have interactions with the fiat world where things are more centralized and you can move things freely from one side to the other. I don't see us as replacing um you know traditional banking rails. I see us as working alongside them in conjunction. Ultimately, you want to be able to as a consumer or a user be able to move funds into your own custody and be able to control them when you want to. And when you need to interact with the rest of the world or need to interact with centralized entities, you can do that as well. So, we're trying to get the best of both worlds in that regard whilst making sure that the user maintains control and privacy when they need it. Yeah, the self-custody question is interesting and I've begun to appreciate it as kind of a cryptonative concept, right? So, I think if you said self-custody to the average, you know, bank user, they would say, "Oh, yeah, I I self-custody, you know, the dollars or whatever in my in my bank account." Um, but it's not true. And you realize that you know when you get uh you know uh h like hacked or someone you know someone gets into your account somehow you get your account compromised or you get auto build right like that's an example of something that's like not not self-custody they're taking your bank they're taking your money out of your account without your kind of consent. Um, also recently I was, you know, I bought World Cup tickets and then had to sell them to buy tickets for a different game. And it's like a a six-w weekek, you know, period to get your money back where, you know, in crypto payments back and forth that that instantaneous, you know, settlement is like very very powerful. Um, how do you bring this idea of self- custody to people, especially given the fact that kind of it doesn't that concept doesn't really exist or or resonate with people in in kind of the the real financial world? >> Yeah, I I think it's one of those things that's should be under the hood. Um, and for a lot of users, they don't they simply don't care because they don't understand it. >> Mhm. But for a lot of users, they do care because they don't trust the relevant government entities to freeze their funds and not because they are doing anything wrong. Just because they're, you know, in in some jurisdictions and in some places those government entities are overreaching. They are um not working ethically and in some cases they're potentially corrupt as well. So people people do value being able to self custody their funds in those markets more than they would do for example in in a lot of Europe and the US. But it's it goes the same way with privacy as well. It's like people don't realize they need it until something bad happens. And we want to make sure that if something does bad happens, our users are protected from it. And it is a feature that is kind of under the hood table stakes foundational rather than something that people um necessarily will be jumping up and down about. >> Mhm. With your background, you guys you could have approached kind of many many problems, right? Where the idea that crypto lacks product effort, knowledge, you know, development is not new. Um, why this particular vertical? Why do you think that your skill set uh, you know, is particularly helpful in in the kind of DeFi abstraction space? >> It's definitely my background in DeFi and traditional finance. So, it's it's more interesting to me. is also I think an area that is more mature compared to some of the other aspects that you you can or other use cases that you can facilitate with blockchains like we've we've got pretty established D5 protocols now with high volumes and deep liquidity is also ties in with the adoption that we're seeing on stable coins these things are all kind of flowing in into and out of each other and >> I feel like it's a really good time to to push that and kind of help to mature it. Or the other point is finance is global, it's impactful and it's full of use cases within it. So although it's um you know a big a big problem to tackle and a big problem to make better, it's you know potentially a very rewarding one from a product perspective to take something that's so complex, something that's so difficult for people to to access that I truly believe is is going to be society beneficial and boil that down into a really accessible user experience where people are going to actually feel the benefit of it and and hopefully change their life for the better. Fantastic. Um, let's talk about specifics. Uh, I know, you know, when you hop onto the defi.com website, uh, a big part of the the kind of branding is this idea of this DeFi ID and this kind of identity, reputation, a reputation score even around around DeFi. Talk to me about what what that looks like. >> Yeah. So, so one of the key tenants that we have in the product is is bringing an identity layer to crypto and to to DeFi in particular. You can, I'm sure you know, if you arrive at one D5 protocol and you um you're a keen user of that and then you go to another D5 protocol and connect and start using that, there's no kind of carryover or reputation of you being a good potentially a good actor. Um >> there's a reason for that because we want it to be permissionless and um and we want everyone to be able to access it. But these concepts that we have in traditional finance around um credit and lending or even even before traditional finance existed, credit and interest existed, people were lending each other, you know, herds of cattle and then returning 100 100 heads of cattle and then a year later they'd be getting 110 back. these these kind of things predate money >> and we haven't actually managed to recreate some of the core concepts around that particularly around things like underolateralized loans on DeFi at the moment if you want to get a mortgage through DeFi it's not really possible unless you've got all of the money for example in in Bitcoin the end goal with this is that we provide people with this reputation score based on the actions that they do on chain and they can selectively disclose certain aspects of their data and their score in order to get better rates and potentially hopefully in the future get things like undercolateralized loans. Um in the same way that you would have a credit score or FICO score to prove your eligibility for certain loans or certain um credit rates, you would be able to do the same onchain. >> Mhm. It's almost like bringing the self- custody concept to something like a credit score. >> Exactly. Yeah. And it it should go with you um along the way. It's it's, you know, portable and whatever DeFi protocol you connect with, you're not just building a a reputation with a certain product. It's a portable identity that if you um if you then move from one D5 protocol to another, you'd be able to hopefully reap the benefits of that. it the the use cases extend past that in terms of like gated membership for community members that have to um you know reach a certain score in order to be able to participate these kind of things. >> You talked earlier about how kind of privacy is important and and crypto broadly needs more privacy features for its users if we expect it to kind of reach mass market penetration. that kind of feels contradictory with the idea of kind of like a a DeFi identity, a portable reputation, you know. Um, talk to me about how these things kind of fit together. >> Yeah. So, I I don't think those two things are necessarily mutually exclusive because the mechanisms that we're looking at to develop these would only disclose the information that you need to when you need it. When you want to keep that information private, you would you would be able to do that. So the the DeFi ID for example is not going to be it's not going to be possible for you to track onchain transactions or onchain balances back to your D5 ID. You'll be able to see receive funds to that D5 ID the same way that you do with like an ENS domain, but you wouldn't be able to then look up my D5 ID and see that I've I've got a certain number of assets in there or I've made certain transactions. And that goes all the way through from the spending on the Visa card to depositing into DeFi protocols. That privacy is maintained maintained throughout. And then where you want to interact with, for example, centralized entities. If you wanted to get um in the future, you might want to get a traditional finance loan that's predicated on your your trust score, your reputation score. then you could selectively disclose that to that single counterparty rather than keeping that as a public um information all the time. >> Interesting. So there's kind of like a merge or two of the the DeFi reputation score and kind of the you know the credit world the fight world maybe or or other kind of institutional worlds as well and all all of it's kind of becoming one ecosystem. >> Yeah. and and this like it will take time, right, for this this to kind of develop and be a trusted thing. But I I hope that that's where things go. I feel like the the credit the undercolateralized loan part of finance is missing from DeFi and it's a big a big hole that we don't really talk about. And it's also a big part of the um the usability. people want to be able to, for example, finance a car or do normal stuff, buy a house, and at the moment, we can't do that um through DeFi or or on chain rails successfully. >> Regarding that kind of uncolateralized piece or or the credit piece, um basically the architecture of of your application is kind of looks like you're kind of having a a universal interface and then you're bringing in kind of products, solid products to some extent. Maybe some products sit on top and are are built out within defi.com. You guys have the reputation score. What do you think that undercolateralized or credit piece will look like? Is it about bringing institutions on chain? Is it about you know coming up with DeFi products and integrating those in or how do you how do you see that playing out? >> Yeah, there's there's multiple routes for that and and as I said it will take some time for it to mature and for we would need to build some partnerships around that. um we have been having some discussions around it but um there's a number of different routes that it could take DeFi first or through traditional finance and traditional credit lenders both um would be feasible but the regulation and the um the the partnerships need to be developed and the maturity of that progressive reputation score needs to to pay out as well >> right I'm sure you've talked to kind of potential users or non-crypto users what have been some of the most surprising uh components of these conversations that you guys have had with users um around kind of like what you need to build or what what the obstacles are for onboarding. >> Yeah, so it's it's been a mix um depending on the cohort of users that you speak to and where they live in particular. Privacy is is something that we hear is um becoming more of a concern for people and it probably wasn't the case maybe 6 to 12 months ago. The other the other kind of key problem for people is around remittances and crossber payments. So we are as as I said earlier we are we are trying to work alongside um fiat rails as well as and providing kind of interoperability so to speak around that. So we are also looking at providing our users with um with bank accounts and traditional financing rails within the the product as well and being able to move funds seamlessly from one side to the other provide FX rates these kind of things as well. So that's something that's definitely very important for people is the crossber remittances and crossber payments. And the the final thing is around UX. I think that from the discussions we've had the existing crypto neo banks maybe haven't gone far enough with abstracting away the kind of cryptonative jargon and complexity of um handling crypto and defi in particular. So we we want to take that a step further and make it feel a lot more uh familiar to people that are using traditional banking apps or um fintech apps. >> Yeah, it's interesting. Often when I have friends or family members come to me about, you know, about buying crypto, most of the time the products I suggest to them are not cryptonative project products. they're kind of, you know, like the Neoank crypto early adopters or or those kind of that kind of ecosystem of of crypto friendly, you know, tradi. Um, why do you think that is? Do that do they have a product advantage and and crypto needs to kind of overcome that or or are they is it easier for them to integrate existing financial rails? >> Yeah, I I think it's a combination of those things. One is that they of they often sacrifice the non-custodial aspects of it. Like if you if you look at some of the the fintexs that you can now access crypto, you're not custodying that crypto. And >> the self- custody can add >> complexity. Although the the underlying infrastructure now and before my current role I was I was working a lot more on infrastructure um around this stuff and you know now that we have smart accounts we have um path key signing we have gas abstraction and chain abstraction features as well it's maturing to a state where you can abstract away a lot of that complexity previously maybe even 6 to 12 months ago a lot of that wasn't wasn't possible um so it's definitely getting there. I think all of the component pieces are there now and it's going to going to be a really exciting period for products like ours to build on top of that and see how far they can take that um simplicity and that UX to kind of give people all the benefits that they see from Robin Hood or or Revolute in a non-custodial um product like ours. Yeah, it's it's funny you say that because a a perennial answer uh I have from founders of why X thing isn't ready or why this adoption hasn't been achieved is around oh the infrastructure isn't there yet the infrastructure isn't there yet. It's interesting that you have this strong take that no, the infrastructure is there. Now it's time to build. Now it's time to cross the chasm. Now it's time to address the the real user need or bring the real feature to the user. So I do think we're at a powerful moment. >> Uh yeah, I I I totally agree and and I think it's it's almost like an excuse, right, to kind of blame the infrastructure. Like there are plenty of projects where you know I I think of like Hyperlquid for example they they built their own infrastructure so that they could provide the user experience that they wanted to. The infrastructure now is caught up and I think if there are gaps you build it and you you solve those problems. Um so for us we are partnering with some some great infrastructure providers. We are also building some really novel kind of pieces of infrastructure as well to bridge some of those gaps and the results should play out in the product itself. And I think it will feel like a really a really pleasant experience for people with low friction um and and high levels of um hopefully adoption because of the the user experience being so familiar to people. I don't think we're going to see some of the drop offs that I have seen in some of the previous, you know, crypto products that I've been building over the last um sort of seven or eight years because of that that UX jump that we've had over the last, I would say, 24 months. >> Obviously, we've talked a lot about product today. Um, you also mentioned kind of self-custody as a selling point for for products like yours. Do you think there's a marketing message that needs to arrive to kind of the broad user base or is it a PR question of, you know, just being associated with crypto, something like that? Is there or or or are we going to be able to bring on these users with just having great product? I would lean more to the latter of that, I think, >> because I think if you say to most people that aren't deep in the trenches of this stuff that, oh, you know, you can you can custody your own Bitcoin. They'd be like, I don't even know what that means. Um, right? >> Why would why would I buy it? Maybe it's even intimidating for people to think think of those things. But for for the more cryptonative people, it is a selling point and and we're not discounting that user group in the same way that you may be, you know, an advanced um equities trader for JP Morgan, you still want a nice experience in um in your fintech app for managing your day-to-day finances, right? So you can still be a proper DeFi DJ genen and go and do your sophisticated looping strategies directly with the protocol, but we want people to also use this for their kind of day-to-day spending um earning habits in the same way that they would rely on their their kind of fintech apps for this. So it's a selling point for them. It's a benefit that's kind of under the hood for the non-crypto native people and um in some cases as well it may be a selling point if they are more aware of things like government overreach or they're more concerned about those things. But for for the lay person in developing or developed economies I think it's going to be um a nice benefit not necessarily a selling point. One of the ways I kind of like look at the crypto industry is we had this like massive speculative bubble and then some interesting technology came out of that and we've we've talked a bunch about it today remittances some of like the you know self-custody infrastructure there's you know a bunch of user experiences that are interesting it seems like the trady world kind of like took those acquired it and I think you're you're on board with this idea and now they're kind of like integrating it where it makes sense but they're leaving all this kind of speculative slot behind. behind. Um, so there's there's kind of like a bullish case for crypto, but maybe it'll all be, you know, taken over by tradi. Meanwhile, the crypto space kind of around sentiment use is at a a relative kind of trough and and people are feeling kind of pretty down. You know, uh, we're seeing protocol winds down, protocol windowns, projects wind down, you know, people getting fired in the industry. How do you rectify this kind of like relative you know structural adoption happening you know and this infrastructural you know growth and then this kind of like sentiment you know all-time low at least that's what it feels like. >> Yeah I the sentiment is low and I I think it's low for maybe speculators and less so for builders. Mhm. >> Because of this this adoption that we're seeing in real world use cases and also the like in particular there's things like the institutional adoption that we're seeing where there's a lot more money flowing on chain now or coming on chain. Like I personally feel really optimistic and really bullish around around this and where things are going. I'm definitely not in a in a down a down period, but that's because I'm less I'm not building a a purely speculation based product. Um I think if >> you are a crypto exchange, it's you know, you're probably seeing a lot lower volumes and and lower usage, but for us, that's that's part of the product. It's not the whole product. And what I'm really excited about is how we kind of bridge that gap from onchain finance and DeFi into the real world. And that feels like that gap is like closing or even closed or at least it's closer than ever. So for me, I feel really really optimistic about it. The the big driver is around the stable coin payments as well like that's seeing meaningful adoption. Um, partners like Visa and Mastercard are obviously providing cryptobacked cards now and you can suddenly spend your crypto and use your crypto across millions of merchants and that's um that's really promising, right? It's it's it's bridging that gap to a real world use case that's outside of speculation into payments. On top of that, there's more innovation that's happening around payments uh in particular like direct wallet payments uh that don't involve a card and aentic payments that are using crypto rails underneath um x402 where where agents are paying for API calls and infrastructure directly from a crypto wallet. That stuff is is super cool, super exciting. is still developing, but like as well as the products, the infrastructure is still developing faster and faster and moving more with with AI in particular. So yeah, from from my perspective, I'm I'm like super happy. >> I'm hearing this sentiment a lot. To me, what will prove the sentiment is if we have kind of like non cryptonative founders coming to these products and using these products to build out, you know, their companies without, you know, having this kind of like ideological attachment or industry attachment with crypto. It's like if this is truly the if if there's truly room to be bullish on crypto as an industry, as a technology, then these founders will come to these products, solutions, technologies as just like the right tool for the job. >> Um we're we're about out of Go ahead. >> I I agree and I I think we're already starting to see that with um companies like Deal, for example, who who are running stable coin payroll. they're a HR payroll company, but they now have stable coin payroll products running. Um, so that's, you know, one indicator and there's many more of of these these people that are um these founders that are seeing the benefit of crypto rails and actually implementing it in their products. >> Crypto is sometimes a volatile, hard, speculative, you know, sometimes like politically charged industry to work into. What keeps you excited and motivated to come back working on defi.com every day? >> Yeah, it's it it can be um like I've certainly I've been working in crypto since 2019, so I've been through the the ringer a couple of times um with a couple of bare markets and some really dark periods and definitely felt like throwing in the towel on on this industry multiple times. But I'm seeing a lot of progression in the products, the infrastructure that we've just spoken about, but also the the regulation and how we're getting more clarity there, how this is becoming more mainstream. I mean, think of um some of the companies now that are launching their own stable coins like you think of PayPal for example, right? when I first started like thinking of these companies as even like looking at crypto or being involved in crypto was like um almost like it's too far ahead. You couldn't even think of it that was like the the state that we wanted to get to. We're now there and we're like okay now what can we do? And it it's is super exciting. So I'm I'm motivated by the progress and the adoption that we're seeing in the industry. Um, I'm less worried about the the speculative side of that. I've been through been through enough kind of ups and downs to ride that out and continue to ride it out. And I think we are we are moving in the right direction. >> Yeah. And it kind of as like a founder, it kind of verifies your thesis where you took, you know, a risk in your career coming here in 2019 and you said, damn, I don't know if PayPal is going to get here, you know, someday. But the fact that it has arrived means that kind of like directionally you took a strong bet and that ma means that directionally you're you know you're you're you know you've kind of proven out your history of of being directionally correct around kind of how this technology comes and is integrated in the world. So so that's very cool. Um Sam, where can people find you? What can they check out around defi.com? >> Yeah, go to um to defi.com sign up for the weight list and you'll be getting more information on that and and early access soon. we're looking to to launch this year. Um, you can find us on X on LinkedIn. You can find me on X. Uh, I think my my handle is uh crypto probman. So, find me there and um I'll be posting more about it and and follow along and we'll we'll be launching in the near future. >> Perfect. Thanks so much for your time today. Really appreciate it. Looking forward to see uh to see what comes. >> Thanks, Jack. Great speaking with you. Just
